title: Global (`:g`) and Vglobal (`:v`)
subtitle: "Run a command on every line that matches."
summary: `:g/pattern/cmd` runs `cmd` on every line matching `pattern`. `:v` inverts (every non-matching line). Combine with `:d` to delete-by-pattern, with `:s` to substitute-by-pattern, with `:norm` to do anything.

blocks.text: `:g/pattern/cmd` is read as: *for every line matching pattern, run cmd*. It's how you do bulk operations gated by a search.

rows: Reverse the file (move every line to top)
On every class declaration, open new line + insert `pass`

blocks.title: How `:g` Works
blocks.text:
Vim does a two-pass algorithm:

  1. Scan the range, mark every line matching the pattern.
  2. Run the command on each marked line, in order, *deleting marks as it goes*.

The two-pass design means a command that deletes lines (`:g/foo/d`) doesn't lose its place — the marks are set before any deletion happens.

`:v/pat/cmd` is exactly `:g!/pat/cmd` — the inversion. Mnemonic: "v = inVerse."

The command after the pattern can be anything: `:d`, `:s/.../.../`, `:m`, `:t`, even `:norm` to inject *normal mode* keys per matched line. That last form is wildly powerful.
`:g/^$/d` is the universally-useful blank-line stripper. `:v/keyword/d` keeps only matching lines (poor man's grep).
